lens.included_activities: US stock or custom upholstered household-furniture manufacturers in the lower-middle-market band that provide recurring or repeat outsourced production, private-label, retailer, designer, dealer, hospitality, warranty, or replenishment work to external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Reupholstery and repair shops, transportation seating, mattresses, nonupholstered furniture, one-off craft production without recurring or repeat customers, captive units, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers include furniture retailers, dealers, designers, private-label brands, hospitality buyers, distributors, and households. Revenue comes from stock collections or custom orders and can include repeat retailer replenishment, private-label programs, options and fabrics, warranty replacement, and after-sale support.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupations and wages for broader NAICS 337100 household and institutional furniture and kitchen cabinet manufacturing, not wage-weighted task exposure for eligible NAICS 337121 firms. I mapped management, design, merchandising, sales, purchasing, order processing, planning, customer support, inspection, and documentation tasks to partial AI exposure while assigning little exposure to cutting, sewing, upholstering, assembly, finishing, packing, and handling. The proxy may overstate exposure because upholstered production is especially labor-intensive, or understate it for private-label and custom firms with more design and order-management work.
a.rationale: The broader comparison group reported 66.03% of employment in production occupations. Upholstered furniture adds highly physical cutting, sewing, padding, upholstering, assembly, and finishing, while AI is more applicable to product visualization, option configuration, demand planning, procurement, marketing, order entry, support, and quality documentation.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task inventory using payroll and time-study data from representative upholstered-furniture firms, split among design, merchandising, sales, office, plant, logistics, and warranty roles, would materially change this estimate.
caveats: The occupation source is broader than the six-digit industry and dated 2016.
The task mapping estimates exposure rather than observed AI use and may not capture differences between stock, custom, and private-label producers.

rho.rationale: Visualization, merchandising content, demand planning, purchasing, order entry, customer support, and document workflows can adopt AI with moderate change. Fabric matching, automated inspection, cutting optimization, production scheduling, and predictive maintenance require clean product data, vision systems, machine integration, and human validation because quality and safety failures are costly.
rho.change_evidence: A survey of eligible firms documenting deployed AI use cases, implementation costs, verified labor hours, forecast accuracy, scrap, rework, returns, and warranty outcomes would materially move this estimate.
caveats: Manufacturing AI evidence establishes feasibility rather than adoption rates in upholstered furniture.
Legacy systems, highly variable materials, visual quality judgments, and small-firm digital maturity are not directly measured.

e.rationale: Retailer replenishment, private-label production, dealer programs, designer relationships, and hospitality orders can qualify as recurring or repeat outsourced work. Branded product-only companies, one-time custom shops, owner-dependent craft operations, captive plants, and firms outside the target earnings band are excluded.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census combining NAICS validation, normalized EBITDA, channel mix, repeat-order share, private-label revenue, customer concentration, owner dependence, and ownership would determine eligibility.
caveats: The official definition confirms stock and custom production but not recurring relationships or transferability.
The assigned firm count uses a machinery margin bridge that may be materially different from upholstered-furniture economics.

s5.rationale: Private furniture manufacturers face succession and consolidation pressures, but no six-digit owner-age or completed-control-transfer series was found. Strategic acquisitions and family succession may qualify, while internal reorganizations and closures without transferable operations do not.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, BizBuySell, and furniture-sector advisers matched to eligible firms, combined with owner-age and succession surveys, would materially change the probability.
caveats: This is bounded judgment without observed industry-specific transfer rates.
Small private transactions, plant acquisitions, and brand-only deals are incompletely disclosed and may not represent control transfers of eligible operations.

q.rationale: Design differentiation, customization, delivery reliability, short lead times, domestic responsiveness, and quality can preserve some productivity benefit. Retailer bargaining, private-label contracts, promotions, comparable products, input-cost transparency, imports, returns, and warranty obligations pass substantial value to customers.
q.change_evidence: Retailer and private-label contracts, price lists, promotional allowances, rebate terms, return and warranty agreements, bid histories, and realized margins before and after productivity changes would materially change retention assumptions.
caveats: No source directly measures commercial retention of AI-enabled benefits.
Retention is likely higher for differentiated custom work than for replenishment programs sold through concentrated retailers.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for the broader furniture and related products sector to grow at 1.0% annually through 2034, not constant-quality demand for eligible US upholstered-furniture manufacturers. I converted the annual rate into a five-year ratio but lowered the base and widened the interval for housing turnover, discretionary spending, retailer inventory cycles, and domestic-versus-import share. The proxy may overstate this industry if cabinets or other furniture lead growth, or understate it if upholstered replacement and customization outperform.
d5.rationale: Upholstered furniture is a durable physical household product, but purchase timing is discretionary and linked to household formation, moving, remodeling, credit, and retailer inventories. Broad real output is projected to grow modestly, while current housing data show ongoing construction volume alongside softer permits. Domestic demand can diverge from total consumer demand because of imports.
d5.change_evidence: Product-level domestic shipment units, retail sell-through, retailer inventories, household formation, home sales, replacement cycles, import volumes, and constant-quality forecasts for upholstered seating would materially tighten this ratio.
caveats: The output projection covers the entire furniture sector rather than NAICS 337121.
Housing units are a driver rather than a direct measure of upholstered-furniture quantity.
Retail inventory corrections and import share can cause domestic production to diverge sharply from final demand.

o.rationale: Year-five demand remains for physical upholstered seating that must be designed, sourced, cut, sewn, assembled, tested, labeled, delivered, and warranted. Software can enable visualization, configuration, and direct retail, but cannot replace the product. Imports and retailer-controlled sourcing can displace a US manufacturer from part of retained demand.
o.change_evidence: Retailer sourcing data, import penetration by upholstered product class, domestic delivery-time advantages, direct-to-consumer adoption, and customer interviews on local customization would materially change this estimate.
caveats: The estimate applies after the demand change in d5 and avoids counting cyclical volume loss twice.
Physical product demand can persist while accountable supply shifts from US lens firms to imported or retailer-controlled products.

narrative.executive_view: Eligible firms are upholstered-furniture manufacturers with repeat retailer, private-label, dealer, designer, or hospitality programs. AI can improve design visualization, merchandising, planning, ordering, support, and inspection, but the production process remains intensely physical. Durable product demand is offset by discretionary timing, channel power, and import displacement risk.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can accelerate concept visualization, option configuration, merchandising content, demand planning, procurement, order entry, customer support, quality documentation, and some vision inspection. Cutting, sewing, padding, upholstering, assembly, finishing, packing, and delivery remain physical and variable-material work.
narrative.value_capture: Customization, design, domestic responsiveness, lead time, and quality support retention. Large retailers and private-label customers can demand lower prices or allowances, while promotions, comparable styles, returns, warranties, and imports increase pass-through.
narrative.firm_availability: The assigned firm population is estimated using a margin bridge that may not fit furniture economics. Eligibility further depends on target earnings, repeat external-customer programs, transferable operations, and manageable concentration, requiring a bottom-up company map.
narrative.demand_durability: Households continue to require physical seating, and flammability testing, labeling, delivery, and warranty preserve operator accountability. Purchase timing is discretionary and linked to moves, housing, credit, and retailer inventory cycles. Broad furniture output is projected to rise modestly, but domestic production can lose share to imports.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key risks are retailer concentration, imports, promotions, freight, fabric and foam costs, inventory corrections, fashion obsolescence, returns, flammability compliance, product liability, and labor-intensive variable-material production. Firm eligibility, domestic demand share, and transfer rates are not observed directly.
narrative.principal_driver: Repeat retailer and private-label programs create recurring demand where AI can improve design, planning, ordering, and customer-service workflows.
narrative.principal_weakness: Highly physical production, strong channel bargaining, discretionary demand, and import substitution constrain implementable and retainable gains.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 337121 Upholstered Household Furniture Manufacturing
sources.supports: Official industry scope covering stock or custom upholstered household furniture and distinguishing reupholstery and other furniture categories.

sources.title: May 2016 Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 337100
sources.supports: Broader furniture and cabinet occupation mix, including the production-occupation share used for the AI-exposure bridge.

sources.title: Artificial Intelligence for Energy: Opportunities for a Modern Grid and Clean Energy Economy
sources.supports: Manufacturing AI applications including automated visual quality control, operations optimization, and predictive maintenance.

sources.title: Industry Employment and Output Projections, 2024 to 2034
sources.supports: Employment and real-output projection for the broader furniture and related product manufacturing sector.

sources.title: Flammable Fabrics Act Business Guidance: Upholstered Furniture
sources.supports: Federal upholstered-furniture flammability testing, labeling, and certification requirements supporting continued accountable product work.

sources.title: Monthly New Residential Construction, June 2026
sources.supports: Current housing permits, starts, and completions used as context for household-furniture demand and housing-cycle uncertainty.
